entries.name: Fosfomycin
entries.memo: Blocks the **first step** of the cell wall (pyruvyltransferase); **single dose** for cystitis, thanks to bladder accumulation.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibits pyruvyltransferase (first reaction of peptidoglycan biosynthesis); reactive analog of phosphoenolpyruvate. Bactericidal.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: pyruvyltransferase (PEP-dependent enzyme).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: small, highly hydrophilic molecule; well absorbed orally; does not bind plasma proteins; rapid renal excretion with sustained accumulation in the bladder; single administration 2-3 g/day orally. Unstable intermediate (phosphonic derivative).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: uncomplicated urinary tract infections (selective indications); broad spectrum Gram+/Gram-.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: well tolerated.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: low resistance rate (especially E. coli).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: interactions with drugs that alter the GI tract (e.g. metoclopramide).

entries.name: Cycloserine
entries.memo: Analog of **D-alanine** (cell wall); second-line antitubercular, **neurotoxic** (seizures, psychosis).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: acts on the first step of peptidoglycan assembly; structural analog of D-alanine; inhibits alanine racemase (L-ala → D-ala) and the ligase that forms the D-ala–D-ala dimer. Broad-spectrum bactericidal.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: alanine racemase + D-ala–D-ala ligase.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: well absorbed orally; good diffusion (CSF, serous cavities, placenta → not safe in pregnancy); 0.5 g BID; slow elimination.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: broad spectrum Gram+/Gram-; second-line antimycobacterial/TB therapy (after isoniazid, rifampicin, ethambutol).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: CNS toxicity (due to high diffusion): headache, agitation, insomnia → tremors, psychosis, seizures.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: no specific data in the documents.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

entries.name: Bacitracin
entries.memo: Blocks bactoprenol; too toxic for systemic use → only **topical use** (Gram+).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: cyclic peptide derivative; inhibits bactoprenol (the phospholipid carrier that transports forming peptidoglycan toward the outside of the cell).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: bactoprenol.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: not completely absorbed orally → exploited for topical action (like neomycin, rifaximin).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: selective Gram+ spectrum; topical action for intestinal infections by susceptible germs (cocci, Gram+ bacilli).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: GI tract (nausea, constipation, vomiting).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: does not cross the outer membrane of Gram-.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: often combined with neomycin (an aminoglycoside).

entries.name: Glycopeptides (class — vancomycin, teicoplanin) and daptomycin (lipopeptide)
entries.memo: Latch onto **D-ala-D-ala** (not the enzyme); for **resistant Gram+**. VanA resistance = D-ala-D-lactate.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: complex cyclic peptides; bind directly to the D-alanine–D-alanine dimer → prevent target recognition by the enzymes (blocking transglycosylase). Bactericidal.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: D-alanine–D-alanine dimer (substrate).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: see individual molecules.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: Gram+: methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), Enterococci, Bacillus anthracis, Clostridium difficile.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: hypersensitivity (see individual drugs).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: VanA phenotype = inducible cross-resistance vancomycin/teicoplanin; constitutive or inducible plasmid.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: caution with combination with aminoglycosides (endocarditis); avoid synergistic toxicity (oto-/nephrotoxicity, e.g. with diuretics).

entries.name: Vancomycin
entries.memo: IV for **MRSA**, **orally for C. difficile** (not absorbed); slow infusion or **'red man syndrome'**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: glycopeptide (binds the D-ala–D-ala dimer).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: D-alanine–D-alanine dimer.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: orally → poor absorption (macrocyclic structure) → intestinal topical action; IV for systemic infections; good distribution, low plasma protein binding, reaches the CNS with inflamed meninges; renal elimination; low t½ → repeated low doses.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: C. difficile pseudomembranous colitis (orally); severe MRSA (IV): septicemia, pneumonia, osteomyelitis, soft tissue infections.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: hypersensitivity (urticaria, anaphylactoid reactions, phlebitis, red man syndrome); nephrotoxicity; ototoxicity.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: MIC increase over the years; VanA.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: caution + aminoglycosides (endocarditis) and + diuretics (ototoxic synergism).

entries.name: Teicoplanin
entries.memo: Like vancomycin but once daily, **no red man syndrome**, less nephrotoxic.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: glycopeptide.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: D-alanine–D-alanine dimer.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: long duration of action, high plasma protein binding → IM as a single daily administration (also IV).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: same as vancomycin; comparable efficacy.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hypersensitivity.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: VanA phenotype (inducible cross-resistance with vancomycin).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: no specific data in the documents.

entries.name: Antimycobacterials (general overview + antitubercular therapy)
entries.memo: **RIPE** regimen (Rifampicin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol): **always in combination**, or resistance.
freetext: Characteristics of mycobacteria: slow growth, sparse activation cycles, intracellular, thick cell wall with mycolic acids (protection), localization in hard-to-reach tissues (bone marrow, liver, lymph nodes), reactivation after latency → long therapies (months), in combination, with multiple mechanisms, to counter rapidly emerging resistance.
First line (fewer side effects): isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide; also ethambutol (less tolerated, especially + isoniazid).
Second line (greater toxicity/resistance): streptomycin and others.
TB regimen: initial phase 2 months = isoniazid + rifampicin + pyrazinamide + ethambutol; maintenance 4 months = isoniazid + rifampicin (pyrazinamide and ethambutol only in the initial phase).
Context: TB among the leading causes of death (third world), increasing cases in Italy, leading cause of death in AIDS; rising resistance to first-line drugs.

entries.name: Isoniazid (INH)
entries.memo: Blocks **mycolic acids**; always give **vitamin B6** (prevents neuropathy); hepatotoxic.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibits the enzymes of mycolic acid biosynthesis → selective action on the mycobacterium; bactericidal.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: enzymes of mycolic acid biosynthesis.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: well absorbed orally; phase II metabolism by acetylation; polymorphism (fast/slow acetylators) → slow acetylators have reduced elimination → ↑ side effects.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: first-choice drug in TB (initial phase + maintenance).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: peripheral neuropathy, nausea, vomiting, constipation, dry mouth (inhibition of cholinergic transmission), psychotic episodes, gynecomastia, hyperglycemia; worse in slow acetylators and in combination with ethambutol.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: development of resistance → use in combination.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: increased toxicity in combination with ethambutol.

entries.name: Ethambutol
entries.memo: '**EThambutol → EYEs**': **optic neuritis** with red-green dyschromatopsia → check visual acuity.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibits the transfer of mycolic acid into the mycobacterial cell wall; selective target; bacteriostatic.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: mycolic acid transfer.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: no details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: first-line TB (initial phase).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: visual disturbances from optic nerve toxicity — optic neuritis, refraction disturbances, red-green color blindness, visual field narrowing, reduced visual acuity; worse if + isoniazid.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: not used in maintenance (potential for adverse effects).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: increased neuropathic toxicity in combination with isoniazid.

entries.name: Rifampicin
entries.memo: Inhibits **RNA polymerase**; **extremely potent CYP inducer** (nullifies the pill!) and turns everything **orange**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: macrocyclic antibiotic; inhibits DNA-dependent RNA polymerase → inhibits RNA synthesis; also active on slow-growing microorganisms.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: DNA-dependent RNA polymerase.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: well absorbed orally; enterohepatic recirculation; INDUCER of cytochromes CYP1A2, 2C9, 2C19, 2D6, 3A4 (already after 7 days); excellent distribution (incl. CNS); turns secretions red (feces, urine, saliva).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: TB (initial phase + maintenance); leprosy (anti-leprotic); other mycobacteria.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: skin eruptions, hepatopathies, GI involvement; rarely hypersensitivity, severe cutaneous reactions, flu-like syndrome.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: increasing resistance (concerning given the drug's manageability/safety).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: in combinations it offers a dual point of attack (different target → ↓ resistance). ⚠️ Enzyme inducer → alters concomitant therapies (caution during the 2+4 months).

entries.name: Pyrazinamide
entries.memo: Active on **dormant bacilli in an acidic environment**; shortens the regimen; signature ADR = **hyperuricemia**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: not clarified; it appears to increase intracellular oxidative damage, concentrate and lower the cellular pH selectively for the mycobacterium, even without replication. Bactericidal even on extremely slow-growing organisms.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: not clarified.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: rapidly absorbed orally. (The most recent among the antitubercular drugs.)

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: TB (initial phase; not in maintenance).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: hepatic toxicity (↑ transaminases), nausea, vomiting, GI alterations, alterations of hemostasis/hematocrit (also concerning), hypersensitivity (IgE or delayed).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: induces resistance → only in combination; less safe in terms of tolerability.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: always in combination (never alone).

entries.name: Second-line antitubercular drugs
entries.memo: For **MDR-TB**: fluoroquinolones, bedaquiline, linezolid; 18-24 months, toxic drugs.

segments.label: Use
segments.value:
resistance/intolerance to first-line drugs or treatment of relapses; greater toxicity profile.
Cycloserine → see dedicated card.
Capreomycin: cyclic peptide, particular mechanism, induces resistance.
Fluoroquinolones: good intracellular distribution; act on DNA gyrase (mechanism different from the "mycolic acid" target); resistance increasing very rapidly.
Kanamycin, amikacin, streptomycin (aminoglycosides): induce resistance; significant toxicity (nephrotoxicity; streptomycin causes irreversible ototoxicity); caution with use alongside isoniazid and other neurotoxic agents.

entries.name: Sulfonamides
entries.memo: Analogs of **PABA** → block folate synthesis (which we don't perform → selective toxicity); risk of **Stevens-Johnson**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: analogs of para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA); inhibit dihydropteroate synthetase (fusion of PABA + pteridine → dihydropteroic acid) in the folate pathway → block the de novo synthesis of tetrahydrofolic acid. Bacteriostatic.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: dihydropteroate synthetase.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: distinction by kinetics. Sulfadiazine/sulfamethoxazole/sulfisoxazole: rapid oral absorption, hepatic metabolism by acetylation, rapid renal excretion → high urinary concentrations. Sulfadoxine: long duration. Sulfaguanidine: protonated in the gastric environment → very low bioavailability → intestinal topical. Silver sulfadiazine: topical. High plasma protein binding.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: broad spectrum (aerobic Gram+/Gram-; common folate pathway). Historically urinary infections (now replaced); respiratory and GI tract. Sulfadoxine + pyrimethamine → malaria (therapy/prophylaxis). Sulfamethoxazole + trimethoprim → nocardiosis, toxoplasmosis. Low cost → developing countries.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: crystalluria (insoluble acetylated metabolites → damage to renal epithelium); severe hematopoietic toxicity; hypersensitivity (similarity to PABA) up to epithelial necrosis and Stevens-Johnson syndrome; hepatopathies, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, headache.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: replaced by drugs with lower toxicity.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: + trimethoprim (synergy, sequential blockade of the folate pathway → co-trimoxazole / Bactrim®). ⚠️ displacement of warfarin, NSAIDs, antiepileptics (high protein binding).

entries.name: Trimethoprim
entries.memo: Blocks **dihydrofolate reductase**; with a sulfonamide it performs a **sequential blockade** (co-trimoxazole/Bactrim).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (downstream reaction) → blocks the synthesis of tetrahydrofolic acid.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: dihydrofolate reductase.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: no details available (used in combination).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: often + a sulfonamide (Bactrim®); cystitis (alternative to fluoroquinolones indicated by the EMA).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: from folate deficiency → megaloblastic anemia, leukopenia, granulocytopenia (resolvable with tetrahydrofolic acid).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: no specific data in the documents.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: + sulfonamides (co-trimoxazole); folate supplementation in case of bone marrow toxicity.

entries.name: Quinolones and Fluoroquinolones
entries.memo: Block **DNA gyrase/topoisomerase IV**; restricted due to **tendinopathies/Achilles rupture** and aortic risk.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibit the enzymes that cut/supercoil the bacterial chromosome → topoisomerase IV (Gram+) and DNA gyrase (Gram-), preventing the supercoiling needed for duplication. Broad spectrum.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: topoisomerase IV (Gram+) / DNA gyrase (Gram-).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: fluoroquinolones are the second generation of quinolones (the prototype is nalidixic acid, today without marketing authorization). The fluorine → excellent bioavailability/distribution (even hard-to-reach tissues), high intracellular Vd, accumulation in phagocytes. Hepatic metabolism, predominantly renal excretion (reduce dose in chronic renal failure/elderly; moxifloxacin tolerated in chronic renal failure). Concentration-dependent, AUC/MIC parameter (>50% for success). Generations: nalidixic → norfloxacin → cipro/levofloxacin → moxifloxacin (also covers atypicals: chlamydiae, legionella, mycoplasma, M. tuberculosis).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: respiratory infections (bronchitis, sinusitis, pneumonia), sexually transmitted, prostatitis, uncomplicated genitourinary, bone (pefloxacin), soft tissues, GI; inpatient and outpatient.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: CONTRAINDICATED < 12 years (connective tissue toxicity, alteration of β1-integrins) and caution in the elderly (arthropathies, tendinitis, Achilles tendon rupture); GI in 20% (incl. C. difficile pseudomembranous colitis); CNS (drowsiness, confusion, delirium, headache, lowering of the proconvulsant threshold — they displace GABA); QT prolongation/torsade de pointes (max moxifloxacin, min ciprofloxacin); alteration of ATP-dependent K channels and glycemia; phototoxicity; hypersensitivity/idiosyncratic (less than β-lactams).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: EMA 2018 restrictions (no prophylaxis; not if safer alternatives are available; not for mild/moderate infections except in case of hypersensitivity to others; not for non-bacterial infections).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: interaction with theophylline and NSAIDs (protein binding; theophylline + FQ → CNS effects). Second line in TB (cf. file Antibiotici-2).

entries.name: Rifampicin (supplement Lec 17 — complete antitubercular profile in file Antibiotici-2)
entries.memo: Not just TB: **meningitis prophylaxis**, brucellosis, leprosy; always in combination (rapid resistance).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibits DNA-dependent RNA polymerase → inhibition of RNA synthesis. Bactericidal.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: DNA-dependent RNA polymerase.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: metabolism inducer (CYP) → reduces the half-life of digoxin, verapamil, ketoconazole, corticosteroids, barbiturates, oral contraceptives; turns secretions red.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: broad spectrum (Gram+, Gram-, meningococcus, Haemophilus influenzae meningitis); TB in combination with isoniazid.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: nausea, vomiting, skin eruption, hepatopathies.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: increasing resistance (cf. file Antibiotici-2).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ potent enzyme inducer → interactions with many cytochrome substrates.

entries.name: Macrolides (class — erythromycin, clarithromycin, roxithromycin, azithromycin)
entries.memo: Bind to the **50S** (king of atypicals: Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia); **↑ QT** and CYP3A4 inhibition.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: bind to the 50S ribosomal subunit (near the peptide exit tunnel) → block translocation and elongation of the nascent polypeptide chain.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: 50S ribosomal subunit.

segments.label: Origin/structure
segments.value: erythromycin (natural), clarithromycin/roxithromycin (semisynthetic), azithromycin (synthetic). 14-C macrocycle (azithromycin 15 C).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: see individual molecules.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: see individual molecules.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: see individual drugs; hypersensitivity rare.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: modification of the ribosomal target site; CROSS-resistance with lincosamides (clindamycin) and streptogramins (nearby sites of action).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: see individual drugs (CYP/P-glycoprotein interactions).

entries.name: Erythromycin
entries.memo: The prototype; **motilin agonist** → cramps/nausea (sometimes used deliberately as a prokinetic).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: macrolide (50S).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: low oral bioavailability, sensitive to acidic pH → esters (ethylsuccinate, estolate, stearate) with high oral bioavailability. Time-dependent. Important inhibitor of CYP3A4.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: narrow spectrum, selective Gram+; motilin-like effect (↑ peristalsis).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: frequent GI effects; hepatotoxicity (especially estolate); interference with K⁺ currents → sudden cardiac death (more frequent than with amoxicillin), especially if + CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g. statins); transient deafness (high doses/prolonged use/elderly/renal failure/hepatic failure).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: cross-resistance macrolides–lincosamides–streptogramins.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ CYP3A4 inhibitor → ↑ concentrations of corticosteroids, digoxin, benzodiazepines; risk with statins.

entries.name: Azithromycin
entries.memo: **Very long half-life** and tissue accumulation → short regimen; **chlamydia in a single dose**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: macrolide (50S); 15 carbon atoms, dibasic structure.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: bioavailability 37% (dibasic structure) but stable in an acidic environment, lipophilic; long half-life, excellent Vd, excellent distribution in polymorphonuclear leukocytes; trapping phenomenon (two basic centers → protonated and trapped at acidic intracellular pH → concentrates in neutrophils) → post-antibiotic effect (release by degranulation at the infection site) → effective even on intracellular bacteria (chlamydiae); effect up to 5 days after the first dose. Concentration-dependent. NOT biotransformed by the liver; renal elimination 10%, 90% unchanged.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: broader spectrum than erythromycin; S. aureus, S. pyogenes, S. pneumoniae, Moraxella catarrhalis, mycoplasma, intracellular; more effective vs Haemophilus influenzae, Neisseriae, chlamydia → genitourinary infections and STIs. Sub-MIC immunomodulatory effects (panbronchiolitis, asthma, respiratory infections, otitis, cystic fibrosis; used in COVID-19 for the cytokine storm — a unique feature). Dosage: 500 mg/day × 3 days or 1 g single administration (STIs).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: lower GI toxicity than the others (but motilin-like effects: GI disturbances, epigastric heaviness, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea); does NOT cause the K⁺/CYP effects of erythromycin.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: MIC increase → reduced success; class cross-resistance.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: absence of the CYP/cardiac interactions of erythromycin.

entries.name: Clarithromycin (note)
entries.memo: Cornerstone of the **anti-H. pylori triple therapy**; strong CYP3A4 inhibitor, prolongs the QT.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: semisynthetic macrolide (50S).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: bioavailability 55%; may interfere (less than erythromycin) with K⁺ currents.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value:
⚠️ inhibits P-glycoprotein → ↑ concentrations of P-gp substrates (e.g. digoxin).
Other aspects: as per the macrolide class.

entries.name: Lincosamides (Clindamycin)
entries.memo: Excellent against **anaerobes**, but it is the **leading cause of C. difficile colitis**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: binds to the 50S subunit; semisynthetic derivative of lincomycin.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: 50S ribosomal subunit.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: intense hepatic metabolism, elimination via feces; penetrates polymorphonuclear granulocytes → reaches hard-to-reach tissues (bones). Topical formulations (gel/cream) for acne.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: intermediate spectrum (Gram+, Gram-), active against anaerobes (clostridia); NOT active against aerobic Gram- (does not cross the outer membrane); osteomyelitis (e.g. from streptococci).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: NOT active against C. difficile, indeed it can cause its pseudomembranous colitis (selection in 10%); nausea, vomiting; neuromuscular junction blockade (caution in myasthenia gravis).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: cross-resistance with macrolides/streptogramins; contraindicated in hepatic/renal alterations and diarrhea.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: systemic use infrequent; no data on specific combinations.

entries.name: Chloramphenicol (CAF)
entries.memo: Very broad spectrum but toxic: **aplastic anemia** and **gray baby syndrome** in the newborn.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: interferes with the 50S subunit → prevents the binding of tRNA to the acceptor site → blocks elongation.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: 50S ribosomal subunit.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: excellent distribution (incl. cerebral → brain abscesses); metabolism by glucuronidation (deficient in newborns).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: broad spectrum (Gram+, Gram-, rickettsiae, mycoplasmas, chlamydiae; bactericidal on Haemophilus, Neisseriae, S. pneumoniae); typhoid fever, bacterial meningitis, brain abscesses, anaerobes, brucellosis, rickettsioses, lymphogranuloma venereum. Reserved for difficult germs / when safer alternatives (tetracyclines, FQ, β-lactams) cannot be used.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: mitochondrial toxicity; hematopoietic toxicity (hypochromic anemia, bone marrow aplasia); gray baby syndrome (newborns, reduced glucuronidation → accumulation; mortality 40%); nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hypersensitivity, angioneurotic edema, peripheral neuritis, optic neuritis, psychiatric disturbances.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: poorly manageable; contraindicated in bone marrow depression and breastfeeding.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ inhibits CYP450 → alters dicoumarol, phenobarbital, phenytoin; CYP inducers reduce its efficacy.

entries.name: Aminoglycosides (class — mechanism, kinetics and toxicity)
entries.memo: Bind to the **30S** (misreading of mRNA → bactericidal); **single dose/day**; **nephro- and ototoxic**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: bind to the 30S subunit with a dual action → inhibit the initiation of protein synthesis AND promote aberrant proteins (misfolded/incomplete) that insert into the membrane/cell wall → alter permeability → lysis. BACTERICIDAL.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: 30S ribosomal subunit.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: hydrophilic; concentration-dependent par excellence; cross the porins of Gram- (easier at 4-5× MIC); ATP- and O₂-dependent entry (→ only aerobes; do not enter eukaryotic cells → ineffective on intracellular organisms). Poor oral absorption (→ intestinal topical); good distribution in interstitial fluids (ascitic, synovial, pleural); systemic use = parenteral. Not biotransformed, 90% excreted unchanged via the kidney; low protein binding; short half-life. Strategy: a single high, intermittent daily peak → maximizes efficacy and reduces accumulation/toxicity; post-antibiotic effect.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: predominantly Gram- (Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella, Enterobacteriaceae, Proteus, E. coli); β-lactamase-producing staphylococci are susceptible; resistant streptococci. (Individual drugs → dedicated card.)

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: low therapeutic index. OTOTOXICITY (accumulation in endolymph/perilymph → VIII pair: early vestibular toxicity — vertigo, nausea, vomiting, nystagmus; cochlear toxicity insidious — first high then low frequencies → irreversible bilateral deafness; tinnitus = early sign). NEPHROTOXICITY (cortical accumulation → tubular damage/acute renal failure; 10% of drug-induced acute renal failure; neomycin the worst; reversible). Neuromuscular junction blockade (↓ acetylcholine release → caution in myasthenia gravis). Hypersensitivity.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: modifying enzymes (acetyl-, phospho-, adenyl-transferases), acquired via plasmids; the semisynthetics (netilmicin, amikacin) resist acetyl-transferases.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: + β-lactams/penicillins (weaken the Gram+ cell wall → favor entry; e.g. endocarditis). ⚠️ avoid with other ototoxic agents and loop diuretics (furosemide, ethacrynic acid); nephrotoxic synergism with NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, cyclosporine, cisplatin.

entries.name: Aminoglycosides — individual drugs
entries.memo: **Amikacin** = reserve for gentamicin-resistant strains; **neomycin** topical only (too toxic).
freetext: Natural origin: streptomycin, gentamicin, tobramycin, kanamycin, neomycin. Semisynthetic: netilmicin, amikacin.
Streptomycin / gentamicin: septicemia, upper urinary tract infections, endocarditis (+ a β-lactam), brucellosis, plague.
Gentamicin: peritonitis, meningitis, pneumonia, Gram+ infections (+ penicillin).
Tobramycin: NOT with penicillins in endocarditis (incompatibility); active against Pseudomonas aeruginosa; ophthalmic eye drops (± glucocorticoids).
Neomycin / paromomycin: topical (not absorbed) → enteric infections, pre-surgical bowel preparation, ↓ ammonemia in hepatic coma; neomycin = severe nephrotoxicity → not systemic.
Amikacin / netilmicin: resistant to acetyl-transferases → gentamicin-resistant bacteria, nosocomial infections.

entries.name: Tetracyclines
entries.memo: Bind to the **30S**; **not under age 8/in pregnancy** (teeth), photosensitivity, **chelate Ca²⁺/Fe** (not with milk).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: insert into the 30S subunit → prevent the binding of tRNA to the acceptor site. Four-ring structure with a divalent-cation chelating motif.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: 30S ribosomal subunit.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: chelation of divalent cations (Ca, Mg) → insoluble precipitates in the gastric environment → zero bioavailability (caution with dairy products/cheeses/antacids); bone tropism (chelate tissue Ca). Natural: tetracycline, oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, demeclocycline; semisynthetic: doxycycline, minocycline.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: broad spectrum (Gram+, Gram-, aerobes, anaerobes, protozoa, spirochetes, rickettsiae, ureaplasmas, mycoplasmas, chlamydiae); rickettsioses, intracellular organisms, brucellosis/cholera, cocci, genitourinary, acne (Propionibacterium acnes), H. pylori (in combination).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADRs)
segments.value: CONTRAINDICATED < 8 years and in pregnancy/breastfeeding (irreversible dental discoloration/hypoplasia, ↓ bone strength; in newborns ↑ intracranial pressure); direct GI toxicity (esophagitis, erosions/ulcers, pancreatitis, hepatitis, pseudomembranous colitis); renal toxicity (interstitial nephritis, acute renal failure — less than aminoglycosides; ⚠️ expired tetracyclines = nephrotoxic); hypersensitivity rare (rash, angioedema); thrombophlebitis, photosensitivity; minocycline → transient vestibular disturbances.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: widespread and easy to induce — efflux pumps (Tet genes, plasmids), ribosomal protection, reduced penetration, enzymatic inhibition.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ❌ never with divalent cations (dairy products, antacids); + other drugs in H. pylori therapy.
